question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol)] Can an unprivileged taker, taking a sell offer (offer.buy=false) with a non-zero takerCallback pointing to a third-party contract that has a standing ERC20 approval to Midnight, under preconditions where that third-party contract implements onBuy returning CALLBACK_SUCCESS unconditionally, trigger take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, thirdPartyCallback, data) causing payer = thirdPartyCallback and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, thirdPartyCallback, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) to drain the third party's tokens, violating the invariant that tokens must only be pulled from an explicit payer who consented to this specific transaction? Proof idea: deploy a contract with standing approval and unconditional CALLBACK_SUCCESS return, fuzz takerCallback = that contract, assert third-party balance decreases without their per-call consent.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + take] Can an attacker who is authorized by victim on Midnight (isAuthorized[victim][attacker] = true) sign an Authorization struct with authorizer=victim, authorized=attackerCallbackContract, isAuthorized=true, nonce=currentNonce, deadline=far_future, submit it to EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then use attackerCallbackContract as the buyerCallback in a buy offer take, causing tokens to be pulled from attackerCallbackContract which the attacker controls, effectively bypassing the explicit payer requirement by laundering authorization through EcrecoverAuthorizer? Proof idea: unit test showing attacker-as-authorized-signer can grant their own callback contract authorization and use it as payer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker, given that victim has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer on Midnight (isAuthorized[victim][EcrecoverAuthorizer]=true) and also authorized attacker (isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true), create a valid Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true} signed by attacker (not victim), submit it to EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, and have it accepted because the signer check allows isAuthorized[authorization.authorizer][signer], thereby granting attacker self-authorization through victim's account without victim's direct signature? Proof idea: fuzz signer != authorizer but isAuthorized[authorizer][signer]=true, assert authorization succeeds and isAuthorized[victim][attacker] is set.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay (Midnight.sol)] Can an unprivileged caller who is authorized by borrower (isAuthorized[borrower][caller]=true) call repay(market, units, borrower, maliciousCallback, data) where maliciousCallback is a contract that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onRepay but does not actually transfer tokens, causing the payer to be set to maliciousCallback and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, maliciousCallback, address(this), units) to pull from maliciousCallback instead of msg.sender, violating the invariant that the explicit payer must be the entity that consented to the repayment? Proof idea: deploy callback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS without transferring, assert repay succeeds with wrong payer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: liquidate (Midnight.sol)] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, repaidUnits, borrower, postMaturityMode, receiver, thirdPartyCallback, data) where thirdPartyCallback is a contract with standing loanToken approval to Midnight and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onLiquidate, causing payer = thirdPartyCallback and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, thirdPartyCallback, address(this), repaidUnits) to drain the third party's tokens without their per-liquidation consent, violating the explicit payer invariant? Proof idea: fuzz callback address to a contract with approval but no per-call consent, assert balance decreases.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) payer derivation] Can a taker taking a buy offer (offer.buy=true) with offer.callback=address(0) and takerCallback=address(0) cause payer = buyer = offer.maker, and if the maker has a standing loanToken approval to Midnight but did not intend to pay in this specific take (e.g., the offer was partially consumed and maker expected callback-based payment), result in tokens being pulled from the maker without their per-transaction callback consent? Proof idea: create buy offer with no callback, partial fill, assert maker's tokens are pulled directly via safeTransferFrom(loanToken, buyer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized nonce race] Can two concurrent submissions of the same signed Authorization (same nonce, same authorizer) result in one succeeding and one reverting with InvalidNonce, and if the first submission is front-run by an attacker who submits a different Authorization with the same nonce (signed by an authorized signer), cause the victim's intended authorization to be permanently blocked while the attacker's authorization is applied? Proof idea: simulate front-run with same nonce, assert victim's authorization is blocked and attacker's is applied.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized chain] Can an attacker who is authorized by victim on Midnight use EcrecoverAuthorizer to sign an Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attackerOwnedContract, isAuthorized=true} and then use attackerOwnedContract as the callback in repay(market, units, victim, attackerOwnedContract, data), causing tokens to be pulled from attackerOwnedContract (which attacker controls) rather than from victim or msg.sender, effectively routing the payer through an attacker-controlled contract? Proof idea: multi-step PoC: authorize via EcrecoverAuthorizer, then call repay with attacker callback as payer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) sell offer sellerCallback] Can a maker of a sell offer (offer.buy=false) set offer.callback to a contract that, during onSell, calls back into Midnight to perform a second take or repay using a different payer, causing the outer take's payer (msg.sender) to be charged while the inner call's payer is a third party, resulting in double-payer confusion or the outer payer being bypassed? Proof idea: deploy sellerCallback that reenters take with different payer, assert outer payer is not double-charged and inner payer is correct.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: flashLoan (Midnight.sol)] Can an unprivileged caller call flashLoan(tokens, assets, thirdPartyCallback, data) where thirdPartyCallback is a contract with standing approvals to Midnight and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onFlashLoan, causing safeTransferFrom(tokens[i], thirdPartyCallback, address(this), assets[i]) to pull from the third party's balance without their per-call consent, violating the explicit payer invariant for flashLoan repayment? Proof idea: fuzz callback to a contract with approval but no per-call consent, assert balance decreases.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized delegation chain] Can an attacker exploit the fact that isAuthorized[authorizer][signer] is checked at submission time (not at signing time) to submit a stale Authorization signed by a signer who was authorized at signing time but has since been revoked, if the submission happens before revocation is mined (front-run revocation), causing the authorization to be applied with a signer who is no longer authorized? Proof idea: sign authorization, revoke signer, front-run revocation with submission, assert authorization is applied with revoked signer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) buyerCallback payer] Can a taker of a sell offer (offer.buy=false) supply takerCallback=attackerContract where attackerContract implements onBuy to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS and has a pre-existing loanToken approval to Midnight, causing payer = attackerContract and both safeTransferFrom(loanToken, attackerContract, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, attackerContract, receiver, sellerAssets) to drain attackerContract's tokens, where attackerContract is a victim contract that the attacker does not own but has induced to hold a standing approval? Proof idea: fuzz takerCallback to victim contract with approval, assert victim's tokens are drained.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + coarse authorization] Can an attacker who is authorized by victim (isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true) use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves (attacker) authorization over victim's account for a new authorized address, then use that new authorized address as a callback payer in take/repay/liquidate, effectively creating an unbounded chain of payer confusion through the coarse-grained authorization model? Proof idea: chain EcrecoverAuthorizer calls to escalate authorization, then use as callback payer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: multicall + take] Can an attacker use multicall to batch a setIsAuthorized call (granting attacker's callback authorization) followed immediately by a take call using that callback as payer, within a single transaction, bypassing any off-chain monitoring or time-delay assumptions about authorization changes, and causing tokens to be pulled from the newly authorized callback without the victim's awareness? Proof idea: multicall([setIsAuthorized(...), take(...)]), assert payer is attacker callback.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) payer = buyerCallback after onBuy] Can a malicious buyerCallback contract, during onBuy execution, call Midnight.setIsAuthorized to revoke its own authorization, then return CALLBACK_SUCCESS, causing the subsequent safeTransferFrom(loanToken, buyerCallback, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) to fail if the token's transferFrom checks authorization, or succeed if it only checks ERC20 allowance, creating an inconsistency between the authorization state and the payer state? Proof idea: deploy callback that revokes its own authorization during onBuy, assert transferFrom behavior.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized signature malleability] Can an attacker exploit ecrecover signature malleability (high-s vs low-s) to submit a second valid signature for the same Authorization struct, bypassing the nonce check by using the same nonce with a malleable signature variant, causing the authorization to be applied twice or the nonce to be incremented unexpectedly? Proof idea: compute malleable signature (flip s, adjust v), submit both, assert nonce behavior and authorization state.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) payer confusion buy vs sell] Can a taker craft an offer where offer.buy=true and offer.callback=address(0) but takerCallback != address(0), causing buyerCallback = offer.callback = address(0) (not takerCallback), so payer = buyer = maker, while the taker expected payer = takerCallback, resulting in the maker's tokens being pulled instead of the taker's callback, violating the taker's explicit payer intent? Proof idea: fuzz offer.buy and callback fields, assert payer derivation matches expected party.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay (Midnight.sol) callback payer bypass] Can an authorized operator (isAuthorized[borrower][operator]=true) call repay(market, units, borrower, operatorOwnedCallback, data) where operatorOwnedCallback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS and has loanToken approved to Midnight, causing payer = operatorOwnedCallback (operator-controlled) instead of msg.sender (operator), effectively allowing the operator to route repayment through any contract they control rather than paying from their own balance, bypassing the explicit payer assumption? Proof idea: operator calls repay with their own callback as payer, assert tokens pulled from callback not operator.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized cross-instance replay] Can a signed Authorization intended for EcrecoverAuthorizer instance A be replayed on EcrecoverAuthorizer instance B (a different deployment with the same MIDNIGHT address), since the domain separator uses block.chainid and address(this) (instance address), but if two instances exist on the same chain with different addresses, the signature is instance-specific and cannot be replayed? Verify: fuzz two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances, assert signature from instance A is rejected by instance B. Proof idea: deploy two instances, sign for A, submit to B, assert InvalidSignature.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) sellerAssets payer split] Can a taker of a sell offer (offer.buy=false) with takerCallback != address(0) cause both safeTransferFrom(loanToken, takerCallback, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, takerCallback, receiver, sellerAssets) to pull from takerCallback, where receiver is a third party, effectively routing the full buyerAssets through takerCallback without takerCallback explicitly approving the split between protocol and receiver? Proof idea: fuzz receiver != takerCallback, assert both transferFrom calls use takerCallback as src.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + take payer chain] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to authorize a malicious callback contract on behalf of a victim (using victim's authorized signer), then immediately take a buy offer where the malicious callback is the buyerCallback (offer.callback), causing tokens to be pulled from the malicious callback which the attacker controls, while the victim's account receives the credit, effectively separating the payer from the credit recipient? Proof idea: EcrecoverAuthorizer grants callback auth, take with callback as payer, assert credit goes to victim but tokens come from attacker callback.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) onBuy callback payer before transferFrom] Can a malicious buyerCallback, during onBuy execution (which occurs before safeTransferFrom), call Midnight.flashLoan to borrow loanTokens, use them to approve Midnight for the buyerAssets amount, then return CALLBACK_SUCCESS, causing the subsequent safeTransferFrom to pull from the callback's flash-borrowed balance, effectively making the callback a zero-capital payer that uses protocol funds to pay itself? Proof idea: deploy callback that flash-borrows during onBuy, assert net token flow is zero-capital for attacker.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized isAuthorized check timing] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer checks isAuthorized[authorization.authorizer][signer] at submission time, not at signing time, to submit an Authorization signed by a signer who was not authorized at signing time but becomes authorized between signing and submission (e.g., victim authorizes signer after signing), causing an authorization to be applied that the victim did not intend to enable? Proof idea: sign authorization before authorization is granted, grant authorization, submit, assert it succeeds.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: liquidate (Midnight.sol) callback payer] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with callback=liquidatorOwnedCallback where the callback, during onLiquidate, calls Midnight.flashLoan to borrow repaidUnits of loanToken, approves Midnight, returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, and then the subsequent safeTransferFrom(loanToken, liquidatorOwnedCallback, address(this), repaidUnits) pulls the flash-borrowed tokens, effectively allowing the liquidator to liquidate with zero upfront capital by using the protocol's own flashLoan as the payer source? Proof idea: liquidate with callback that flash-borrows repaidUnits, assert net capital requirement is zero.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) payer = msg.sender when buyerCallback=0 and !offer.buy] Can a taker of a sell offer (offer.buy=false) with takerCallback=address(0) cause payer = msg.sender, but if msg.sender is an authorized operator (not the taker), the operator's tokens are pulled instead of the taker's tokens, violating the assumption that the taker (not the operator) should be the payer? Proof idea: operator calls take on behalf of taker with no callback, assert operator's tokens are pulled not taker's.
